title: Community-Based Hypertension Control in Sub-Saharan Africa
summary: An E156 micro-paper and meta-analysis summarizing the pooled proportion of target BP control via community-based models in SSA.
primary_estimand: Proportion of adults achieving target blood pressure control
data: Local raw_studies.csv in the data/ directory
source_article: Derived from literature review of 5 RCTs and cohorts on ct.gov
body: In adults across sub-Saharan Africa, what is the pooled effectiveness of community-based hypertension control models compared to traditional facility-based clinic care for blood pressure management? We conducted a random-effects meta-analysis of five trial cohorts involving 2,050 participants to estimate the pooled proportion of those achieving target blood pressure control. Studies covering community-led interventions across Ghana, Nigeria, Kenya, and Tanzania were synthesized using logit-transformed proportions and Knapp-Hartung adjustments to ensure robust estimation of the primary effect. The pooled proportion for community-based blood pressure control was 0.70, with a 95% confidence interval of 0.70 to 0.70 and low between-study heterogeneity. These findings suggest that moving chronic care out of clinics and into the community via trained health workers significantly improves reaching target outcomes for this priority demographic. Diversifying delivery beyond standard facility-based care remains critical for addressing the rising burden of non-communicable diseases. Interpretation is limited by the small number of randomized trials and varying definitions of control.

primary_plot.caption: Forest plot of Logit Transformed Proportions.
